Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time on weekends to catch up, but it rarely worked out the way I planned. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me absorb information better than just reading. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I could squeeze it in between everything else going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd cram before a deadline. I wanted to be more consistent, but life kept throwing curveballs at me. Sometimes I'd plan to study at the same time each week, but those plans fell apart more often than not.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an email reminder would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. I also logged in when classmates mentioned something in our peer support group that I needed to look at. It wasn't really a daily habit for me—more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester when I tried to make it a habit to check the LMS every morning with my coffee. That lasted maybe two weeks before things got hectic again. The peer support group actually helped because they'd message me about new content, which reminded me to log in. But I never really got to a point where it felt automatic or natural.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the information. As an auditory learner, hearing explanations worked so much better than reading dense text. The discussion forums were also helpful when my peers would break down concepts in simpler terms. Those peer interactions, even though I only had a few, made complex topics click for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just piled up and felt overwhelming, so I kept putting them off. I also didn't engage much with the quizzes until right before exams because they felt like extra pressure. Looking back, I probably should have used them more for practice, but at the time they felt like just another thing on my endless to-do list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If someone in my peer group said something was helpful, I'd prioritize it. I also valued anything I could listen to rather than read—that made it feel more accessible to me. If it felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. Required ones got my attention because they directly affected my grade, but optional ones always got pushed to the bottom of my list. There was guilt around not doing them, especially when peers mentioned they were helpful. But with only about 10 hours a week to study, I had to make tough choices about what to prioritize.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download the lecture audio and listen to it on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had a study notebook where I'd write out concepts after listening to lectures. Sometimes classmates would share their notes with me, and I'd study from those instead of going back into the system.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd replay downloaded lectures three or four times while doing housework, really trying to understand the material. I'd also meet up with peers from my support group to discuss concepts in person, which was incredibly valuable. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it downloaded correctly, then I'd listen to it offline multiple times over the following days. The LMS would only show that one initial view, but I might have engaged with that content five or six times. It was just more practical for me to study on the go rather than being tied to my computer.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. I was dealing with some family stuff that took up a lot of my mental energy, and the course material was getting more complex. My attendance dropped because I couldn't make it to classes, and I fell behind on assignments. Even though I wanted to engage, I just couldn't find the capacity. It felt like I was drowning and couldn't catch my breath.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities were huge—I had work and family obligations that didn't pause for my coursework. There were weeks when fatigue made it impossible to focus, so even when I tried to study, nothing stuck. The confusion from falling behind made it even harder to jump back in.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to everything else I needed to deal with. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. The activity was there, but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The thought of starting them felt overwhelming, especially when I was already behind. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy or time, but tomorrow never came the way I hoped. It wasn't laziness—it was more like paralysis from feeling so far behind that I didn't know where to start.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only real motivator to use the LMS intensively. I'd have periods of low activity, then a spike right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated. The problem was that by the time deadlines hit, I often didn't have enough time to do quality work. I was always playing catch-up instead of staying ahead.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always in productive ways. Positive feedback motivated me to keep trying, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of assignment altogether. The peer support helped here because my classmates would encourage me to use feedback constructively. Without that support, I might have disengaged even more after tough critiques.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work well for someone like me with an unpredictable schedule. Weekly modules piled up quickly when I fell behind. I wished there was more flexibility in how and when I could complete things. The structure assumed a level of consistency that I just couldn't maintain given everything else in my life.
Q19: Having audio versions of lectures made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. But the heavy reliance on reading materials and written assignments made things harder. The discussion forums were good in theory, but they felt intimidating to post in regularly. I think more varied ways to demonstrate learning would have helped me show what I actually understood.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. They might be researching, watching videos, reading discussions—genuinely trying to learn—but then freeze when it comes to submitting something. Fear of failure or perfectionism can create that gap between activity and output. I've definitely been there myself.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat, though my performance wasn't great either. But I know that a lot of my real learning happened offline, away from the LMS. Someone could be studying effectively using downloaded materials, textbooks, or study groups without logging in much. The LMS only captures one slice of how people engage with course content.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like when you're most active and what you click on. It can track completion of specific tasks and time spent on the platform. But it completely misses the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. It also misses all the offline learning, peer conversations, and personal struggles that shape how someone engages.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and making an effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and curious. Sometimes I was engaged even when my numbers looked bad, and sometimes I was disengaged even when I was technically active. It's more about the internal commitment than the external metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while managing family responsibilities, or the emotional support I got from my peer group that kept me from giving up entirely. My resilience might be low on paper, but I kept showing up in ways the data can't measure. The surprise I felt when I still failed despite trying—that's not in any log either.
